title: 10 best herbal teas for stress
intro_desc: Herbal teas for stress are caffeine-free infusions made from various dried herbs, flowers, and spices, which have calming and soothing properties that help to alleviate stress and anxiety. Examples of such herbs include chamomile, known for its relaxing effects; lemon balm, which has been shown to reduce anxiety and improve mood; and passionflower, which may help to lower blood pressure and promote better sleep. By incorporating these teas into a daily routine, individuals may experience a reduction in stress levels, improved mental clarity, and an overall sense of well-being. This can lead to better performance at work, improved relationships, and a more balanced and fulfilling lifestyle.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Sage tea helps with stress because it contains active compounds like rosmarinic acid and various flavonoids, which have been shown to reduce anxiety and promote relaxation. The soothing properties of sage may be attributed to its ability to act on certain receptors in the brain that help regulate mood and stress responses. Furthermore, sage tea is a natural source of antioxidants that can help protect the body from the damaging effects of stress. By drinking sage tea, individuals may experience a calming effect, making it an ideal beverage for those seeking a natural way to alleviate stress and promote overall well-being.
remedy_properties: Nervine: Sage tea has a calming effect on the nervous system, helping to reduce stress and anxiety (active constituents
Adaptogenic: Sage exhibits adaptogenic properties, which help the body better cope with stress by supporting adrenal function and balancing hormones (active constituents
Antioxidant: Sage tea contains antioxidants that help protect the body from oxidative stress caused by chronic stress and anxiety (active constituents
Antidepressant: Sage has been shown to have antidepressant-like effects, which can help alleviate symptoms of stress and anxiety (active constituents
Improves Cognitive Function: Sage tea can enhance cognitive function, including memory and attention, which can be affected by stress and anxiety (active constituents
remedy_recipe: Boil 8 ounces of water in a kettle or pot for 5-10 minutes.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried sage leaves to a teapot or infuser.
Pour the hot water over the sage leaves and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the used sage leaves.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired, and enjoy.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the sage plant are commonly used in teas for stress due to their calming and soothing properties.
Flowers: Sage flowers are used in teas for stress because they may help to reduce anxiety and promote relaxation.
Stems: The stems of the sage plant can be used in teas for stress as they are believed to have a calming effect on the nervous system.
Seeds: Sage seeds are sometimes included in teas for stress due to their potential to improve mood and reduce feelings of stress and anxiety.
Roots: The roots of the sage plant are used in teas for stress because they may have a sedative effect, helping to promote relaxation and reduce stress.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm tea helps with stress because it contains rosmarinic acid and eugenol, which have been found to have calming effects on the body. These compounds help to reduce anxiety and promote relaxation by acting on the GABA receptors in the brain. Additionally, lemon balm has been shown to have a positive effect on mood, reducing symptoms of stress and promoting a sense of well-being. Drinking lemon balm tea can also help to soothe the digestive system, which can become upset during times of stress. Overall, lemon balm tea is a natural and effective way to help manage stress and promote relaxation.
remedy_properties: Anxiolytic effect: Lemon balm tea has a calming effect on the nervous system, reducing anxiety and promoting relaxation, due to the presence of rosmarinic acid and citral.
Mild sedative properties: Lemon balm tea can help improve sleep quality and reduce insomnia, as it contains antioxidants like eugenol and flavonoids that have sedative effects.

Neuroprotective properties: Lemon balm tea helps protect the brain from stress-induced damage by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation, thanks to its high content of rosmarinic acid and caffeic acid.

Cognitive enhancement: Lemon balm tea can improve memory and attention, helping to counteract the negative effects of stress on cognitive function, due to the presence of rosmarinic acid and terpene alcohols.

Adaptogenic properties: Lemon balm tea helps the body adapt to stress by regulating cortisol levels and supporting the adrenal glands, which is attributed to its rich supply of antioxidants and essential oils.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 1-2 teaspoons of dried lemon balm leaves or 2-3 tablespoons of fresh lemon balm leaves.
Boil 8 ounces of water and let it cool for 1-2 minutes.
Place the lemon balm leaves in a tea infuser or teapot and pour the hot water over them.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-7 minutes.
Remove the lemon balm leaves and enjoy the hot or cooled tea.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of lemon balm are commonly used in teas for stress due to their high concentration of rosmarinic acid, which has been shown to have calming effects.
Flowers: The flowers of lemon balm contain antioxidants and essential oils that can help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation.
Stems: While not as potent as the leaves or flowers, the stems of lemon balm can still contribute to the calming effects of a stress-relieving tea.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: German chamomile tea helps with stress because it contains the antioxidant apigenin, which binds to certain receptors in the brain that help decrease anxiety and initiate sleep. Furthermore, its soothing aroma and warm temperature have a calming effect on the body, promoting relaxation and reducing stress levels. Additionally, chamomile tea has been shown to promote glycine, a neurotransmitter that acts as a mild sedative, contributing to its stress-relieving properties. Overall, incorporating German chamomile tea into your daily routine can help manage stress and promote a sense of tranquility.
remedy_properties: Anti-anxiety properties: German chamomile tea contains the flavonoid apigenin, which binds to benzodiazepine receptors in the brain, helping to reduce anxiety and promote relaxation.
Mild sedative effects: The plant's essential oils, including bisabolol and matricin, contribute to the tea's calming effects, helping to soothe nerves and improve sleep quality.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Chamomile tea's ability to reduce inflammation, thanks to its high levels of antioxidants like quercetin and apigenin, can help alleviate stress-related tension and muscle pain.
Antispasmodic properties: German chamomile tea can help relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing stress-induced digestive issues such as bloating, cramps, and indigestion.

Immunomodulatory properties: By supporting a healthy immune response, chamomile tea can help the body better cope with stress and reduce the severity of stress-related symptoms. This effect is attributed to various active constituents, including bisabolol, apigenin, and alpha-bisabolol oxides A and B.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 2-3 teaspoons of dried German chamomile flowers and place them in a tea infuser or teapot.
Boil water and pour it over the chamomile flowers, ensuring they are fully submerged.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-10 minutes to extract the beneficial compounds.
Remove the tea infuser or strain the tea to discard the spent flowers.
Serve the herbal tea warm, optionally adding honey or lemon to taste, and enjoy its calming effects on stress.
remedy_parts: Flowers: German chamomile flowers are rich in calming compounds, such as apigenin and luteolin, which help reduce stress and promote relaxation.
Leaves: The leaves of German chamomile contain flavonoids and terpenoids that have a soothing effect, making them suitable for stress relief teas.
Seeds: German chamomile seeds are a source of essential oils, like chamazulene, which have anti-inflammatory and calming properties, aiding in stress reduction.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: White tea helps with stress because it contains high levels of antioxidants, which can help to reduce the production of cortisol, a hormone associated with stress. Additionally, white tea is a natural source of the amino acid L-theanine, which has been shown to have a calming effect on the mind and body. The gentle, soothing taste of white tea can also promote relaxation and help to reduce feelings of anxiety. Drinking white tea regularly can contribute to a overall sense of well-being and a more balanced response to stress.
remedy_properties: Anti-oxidative properties: White tea contains high levels of antioxidants, such as flavonoids and polyphenols, which help reduce oxidative stress and protect cells from damage caused by stress.

Anti-inflammatory properties: White tea has anti-inflammatory compounds, such as catechins and epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), which can help reduce inflammation and alleviate stress-induced symptoms.
Adaptogenic properties: White tea contains adaptogenic compounds, such as amino acids and L-theanine, which can help the body adapt to stress and promote relaxation.
Neuroprotective properties: White tea contains neuroprotective compounds, such as caffeine and theaflavins, which can help protect the brain from stress-induced damage and improve cognitive function.
Anxiolytic properties: White tea contains anxiolytic compounds, such as GABA and theanine, which can help reduce anxiety and promote a sense of calm and relaxation.
remedy_recipe: Boil 2 cups of water and let it cool to 170-185°F, as boiling water can damage the tea's delicate properties.
In a teapot or infuser, place 1-2 teaspoons of high-quality white tea leaves, preferably a stress-relieving blend with herbs like chamomile, lavender, or lemon balm.
Pour the hot water over the tea leaves and steep for 3-5 minutes, allowing the flavors and stress-reducing compounds to infuse.
Remove the tea leaves and enjoy the soothing herbal white tea, preferably in a quiet, relaxing environment.
Drink 2-3 cups daily for optimal stress-relieving benefits.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Contain high levels of antioxidants that can help reduce stress and anxiety.
Buds: Rich in flavonoids and catechins, which have calming effects on the nervous system.
Flowers: Have a soothing effect on the mind and body, making them ideal for reducing stress.
Stems: Contain beneficial compounds that can help alleviate symptoms of stress and promote relaxation.
Roots: Known for their anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce stress-related symptoms.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maypop tea helps with stress because it contains passive phytochemicals, particularly chrysin and isovitexin, which have been found to have a calming effect on the body. These compounds may increase the levels of certain neurotransmitters, such as gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), that help regulate anxiety and promote relaxation. Furthermore, maypop tea has been traditionally used as a mild sedative, making it a popular choice for those seeking natural relief from stress and anxiety. Its soothing properties can contribute to a sense of tranquility and improved mental well-being.
remedy_properties: Anti-anxiety: Maypop tea contains active constituents such as passionflower alkaloids and flavonoids, which have been shown to help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation, making it an effective natural remedy for stress.
Sedative: The sedative properties of maypop tea, attributed to the presence of harman alkaloids, can help alleviate symptoms of stress-related insomnia and improve overall sleep quality.
Nervine: As a nervine, maypop tea can help soothe and tonify the nervous system, reducing the negative impact of stress on the body and promoting overall well-being.

Adaptogenic: Maypop tea has adaptogenic properties that can help the body better cope with stress by supporting adrenal function and regulating cortisol levels, a hormone closely associated with stress.

Antioxidant: The antioxidant properties of maypop tea, resulting from its flavonoid content, can help protect the body from oxidative stress caused by chronic stress and inflammation, thus supporting overall health and well-being.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 1-2 teaspoons of dried maypop (Passiflora incarnata) herb for every 8 ounces of water.
Pour boiling water over the herb and let it steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the herb material.
Optionally, add honey or lemon to taste.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day as needed for stress relief.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in antioxidants, maypop leaves are often used to brew calming teas that help reduce stress and anxiety.
Flowers: Containing anti-inflammatory properties, maypop flowers can be used in teas to alleviate stress-related symptoms and promote relaxation.
Seeds: High in essential fatty acids, maypop seeds can be used to make soothing teas that help combat stress and support overall mental well-being.
Rhizomes: Packed with stress-relieving compounds, maypop rhizomes are commonly used in teas to reduce stress and promote a sense of tranquility.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Skullcap tea helps with stress because it contains beneficial compounds, such as flavonoids and saponins, that have been shown to have calming and relaxing effects on the body. These compounds can help to reduce anxiety and promote a sense of calm, making it an ideal beverage for those experiencing stress. Additionally, skullcap tea is caffeine-free, making it a gentle and soothing choice for relaxation. Its pleasant taste and aroma can also help to create a peaceful and calming environment, further aiding in stress reduction. Drinking skullcap tea can be a simple and natural way to help manage stress and promote overall well-being.
remedy_properties: Nervine: Skullcap tea has a calming effect on the nervous system, which is important for stress as it can help to reduce feelings of anxiety and promote relaxation. This property is due to the presence of flavonoids, such as scutellarin, which have been shown to have anxiolytic effects.

Adaptogenic: Skullcap tea can help the body adapt to stress by supporting adrenal function, which is important for stress management as it can help to regulate cortisol levels and reduce the negative effects of chronic stress. The active constituents responsible for this property include triterpene saponins and flavonoids.

Anti-inflammatory: Skullcap tea has anti-inflammatory properties, which are important for stress as chronic inflammation can exacerbate the symptoms of stress and contribute to the development of stress-related disorders. The anti-inflammatory effects of skullcap tea are attributed to the presence of flavonoids and phenolic acids.

Antioxidant: Skullcap tea has antioxidant properties, which are important for stress as chronic stress can lead to oxidative stress and damage to cells and tissues. The antioxidant effects of skullcap tea are due to the presence of flavonoids, phenolic acids, and other polyphenols.

Anxiolytic: Skullcap tea has anxiolytic properties, which are important for stress as they can help to reduce feelings of anxiety and promote relaxation. The anxiolytic effects of skullcap tea are attributed to the presence of flavonoids, such as scutellarin, which have been shown to have anxiolytic effects.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 2 teaspoons of dried skullcap herb, carefully selecting only the leaves and flowers for optimal taste and potency.
Pour 8 ounces of boiling water over the skullcap herb, allowing it to steep for 10-15 minutes to extract the beneficial compounds.
Strain the tea to remove the herb, ensuring a smooth and enjoyable drinking experience.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired, for added sweetness or tang.
Sip the herbal skullcap tea slowly, allowing the soothing properties to help alleviate stress and promote relaxation.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in soothing compounds, the leaves of skullcap are commonly used to brew calming teas for stress relief.
Flowers: Packed with relaxing properties, skullcap flowers are used in medicinal teas to help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation.
Aerial parts: Not included, as they are not used in making skullcap teas for stress.
Roots: Containing high levels of antioxidants, skullcap roots are used in tea blends to combat stress-induced damage and promote overall well-being.
Stems: Infused in teas, skullcap stems provide a gentle, balancing effect, helping to alleviate feelings of stress and tension.
Rhizomes: Not used, as skullcap rhizomes do not have significant stress-relieving properties.
Seeds: Not used, as skullcap seeds are not typically used in medicinal teas for stress.
Buds: Not used, as skullcap buds are not commonly used in teas for stress relief.
Bark: Not used, as skullcap bark is not typically used in medicinal teas for stress.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Valerian tea helps with stress because it contains valerenic acid and other compounds that may have a calming effect on the brain and nervous system. These compounds may increase the amount of a neurotransmitter called gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) in the brain, which has a relaxing effect. Additionally, valerian tea has been shown to promote sleep and improve sleep quality, which can help reduce stress and anxiety. Furthermore, the act of drinking tea itself can be a soothing and mindful experience, providing an opportunity to take a break and relax.
remedy_properties: Anxiolytic property: Valerian tea can help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation by increasing the levels of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) in the brain, which has a calming effect on the nervous system.

Sedative property: The tea has sedative effects that can help improve sleep quality and reduce insomnia, which are common symptoms of stress, due to the presence of valerenic acid and other sedative compounds.
Antidepressant property: Valerian tea may help alleviate depressive symptoms associated with stress by modulating neurotransmitter levels, including serotonin and dopamine.
Muscle relaxant property: The tea can help relieve muscle tension and spasms that often accompany stress, thanks to its content of valepotriates and other muscle-relaxing constituents.
Neuroprotective property: Valerian tea's antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties can help protect the brain from stress-induced damage, supporting overall cognitive health and well-being.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 1 teaspoon of dried valerian root in a tea infuser or teapot with a built-in strainer.
Pour 8 ounces of boiling water over the valerian root and let it steep for 5-10 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or cover the teapot to prevent the tea from becoming bitter.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day as needed for stress relief.
remedy_parts: Roots: Valerian roots are commonly used in medicinal teas for stress due to their high concentration of active compounds, including valerenic acid, which has a calming effect on the nervous system.
Flowers: Valerian flowers are sometimes included in teas for stress, as they contain volatile oils that can help promote relaxation and improve sleep quality.
Leaves: Valerian leaves are used in some medicinal teas for stress, as they contain antioxidants and other compounds that can help reduce anxiety and promote a sense of calm.
Seeds: Valerian seeds are not commonly used in teas for stress, but they do contain small amounts of valepotriates, which have been shown to have sedative and muscle relaxant properties.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Humulus lupulus tea, also known as hops tea, helps with stress due to its calming and soothing properties. The tea is made from the flowers of the hop plant, which contains various compounds including bitter acids and essential oils. These compounds have been shown to have a sedative effect on the body, helping to promote relaxation and reduce anxiety. Additionally, hops tea is caffeine-free, making it an ideal choice for those looking for a natural way to unwind and reduce stress without the stimulating effects of caffeine. Drinking humulus lupulus tea can help create a sense of calm and tranquility, making it a simple and effective way to manage stress and promote overall well-being.
remedy_properties: Anxiolytic: Humulus lupulus tea has a calming effect on the nervous system, reducing anxiety and promoting relaxation, due to its content of α- and β-acids, which interact with GABA receptors.
Sedative: The tea can help improve sleep quality and reduce insomnia, as it contains compounds like phytol and myrcene that have sedative properties.
Antidepressant: Humulus lupulus tea can help alleviate symptoms of depression by modulating neurotransmitter levels, thanks to its content of essential oils and prenylated flavonoids.
Adaptogenic: The tea helps the body adapt to stress by supporting adrenal function and balancing hormones, due to the presence of humulones, lupulones, and polyphenols.

Neuroprotective: Humulus lupulus tea protects the brain from stress-induced damage and promotes cognitive health, partly because of its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory constituents, such as xanthohumol and isoxanthohumol.
remedy_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried hops (Humulus lupulus) flowers to a tea infuser.
Pour the boiling water over the hops in a teapot or mug.
Steep the tea for 5-10 minutes, depending on desired strength.
Remove the tea infuser and enjoy the herbal tea while it's still warm.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Humulus lupulus flowers, also known as hops, contain oils and acids that have a calming effect on the nervous system, making them ideal for stress relief.
Leaves: The leaves of the humulus lupulus plant contain flavonoids and volatile oils that have a soothing effect on the body, making them useful for reducing stress and anxiety.
Stems: The stems of the humulus lupulus plant contain compounds that have a sedative effect on the body, making them helpful for promoting relaxation and reducing stress.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of the humulus lupulus plant contain active compounds that have a calming effect on the nervous system, making them useful for managing stress and anxiety.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ginger tea helps with stress because it contains powerful compounds like gingerols and shogaols that have a calming effect on the body. These compounds help to reduce inflammation and promote relaxation, which can alleviate feelings of stress and anxiety. Additionally, ginger tea has been shown to improve digestion and reduce nausea, which can also contribute to feelings of stress and discomfort. Furthermore, the warm and soothing nature of ginger tea can help to create a sense of calm and tranquility, making it an ideal beverage to enjoy during times of stress.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Ginger tea contains gingerols and shogaols that help reduce inflammation, which can alleviate stress-related muscle tension and pain.
Anxiolytic: Ginger tea has calming properties due to its antioxidant and adaptogenic compounds, which can help manage stress and anxiety levels.
Nausea reduction: Ginger tea's 6-gingerol and 6-shogaol help alleviate nausea and vomiting, common symptoms of stress and anxiety.
Improved digestion: Ginger tea stimulates digestive enzymes, promoting healthy digestion and absorption, which can be negatively affected by stress.
Blood sugar regulation: Ginger tea contains active compounds that help regulate blood sugar levels, preventing stress-induced spikes and crashes.
remedy_recipe: Peel and chop 1 inch of fresh ginger root into small pieces, then place in a tea infuser or loose in a teapot.
Boil 4 cups of water and pour over the ginger, letting it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Add 1 tablespoon of dried chamomile flowers or 2 chamomile tea bags to the ginger tea and let it steep for an additional 5 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or tea bags and stir in 1-2 teaspoons of honey, to taste.
Serve the hot ginger tea immediately, optionally garnishing with a lemon slice or a cinnamon stick.
remedy_parts: Roots: Ginger roots are commonly used in teas to help reduce stress and anxiety due to their calming and soothing properties.
Rhizomes: Ginger rhizomes are rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, making them ideal for reducing stress-related inflammation and oxidative damage.
Leaves: Ginger leaves contain high levels of essential oils, which have been shown to have a calming effect on the mind and body, making them useful for stress relief.
Flowers: Ginger flowers are known for their calming and relaxing properties, making them a popular addition to teas designed to reduce stress and promote relaxation.
Seeds: Ginger seeds are rich in nutrients and antioxidants, which can help to reduce stress and anxiety by supporting overall health and well-being.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Cardamom tea helps with stress because it contains antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce the body's stress response. The warm and soothing nature of the tea can also have a calming effect on the mind and body. Additionally, cardamom tea is known to promote digestion and improve blood circulation, both of which can help alleviate symptoms of stress and anxiety. Furthermore, the aroma of cardamom has been shown to have a positive impact on mood and mental well-being, making it an ideal beverage to enjoy during times of stress.
remedy_properties: Anti-oxidant properties: Cardamom tea contains antioxidants such as caffeic acid and quercetin which help to reduce oxidative stress in the body, thus promoting relaxation and reducing anxiety.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Cardamom tea has anti-inflammatory compounds such as limonene and alpha-pinene which help to reduce inflammation and swelling, thus alleviating stress-related symptoms such as headaches and muscle tension.
Calming properties: Cardamom tea contains volatile oils such as cineole and borneol which have calming and soothing effects on the mind and body, thus reducing stress and promoting relaxation.

Digestive properties: Cardamom tea has carminative and digestive properties which help to relieve digestive issues such as bloating, gas and indigestion, thus reducing stress related to digestive discomfort.
Mood-enhancing properties: Cardamom tea contains compounds such as linalool and geraniol which have mood-enhancing effects, thus reducing stress and promoting a sense of well-being.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 cup of water and 3 cracked cardamom pods, allowing the mixture to steep for 10 minutes.
Meanwhile, warm 1 cup of milk in a separate pot.
Strain the cardamom tea into a cup, discarding the solids.
Combine the strained tea with the warmed milk.
Sweeten with honey to taste, if desired, and enjoy.
remedy_parts: Seeds: Cardamom seeds are commonly used in medicinal teas for stress due to their calming and soothing properties.
Flowers: The flowers of the cardamom plant are used in teas to help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation.
Leaves: Cardamom leaves are often used in tea blends to help alleviate symptoms of stress and promote mental clarity.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of the cardamom plant contain compounds that can help reduce stress and promote a sense of well-being.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat stress with herbal teas is by incorporating them into a daily routine. Certain herbs, like chamomile, lemon balm, and passionflower, possess calming properties that help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation. Steep a tea bag or a teaspoon of loose herbs in hot water for several minutes, then drink slowly, savoring the aroma and warmth. Regular consumption, along with stress management techniques such as meditation or deep breathing, enhances their soothing effects. However, it's important to consult a healthcare professional if stress persists or intensifies, as herbal remedies might interact with prescribed medications.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to stress that are treated with herbal teas include anxiety, insomnia, and digestive problems. Chamomile tea is known for its calming properties and can help reduce anxiety and promote sleep. Peppermint tea aids in digestion and can alleviate symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome. Lemon balm tea can also reduce anxiety and promote sleep, while ginger tea can help soothe an upset stomach and reduce nausea. Herbal teas are a natural and caffeine-free way to manage symptoms of stress and other related ailments. However, it is important to consult with a healthcare provider before starting any new herbal remedies.
